model_narrative: We are synthesizing a distillation column sequence by deciding which candidate columns to build out of a superstructure. This is the master problem of a Benders decomposition: for each candidate column we choose whether or not to build it, and we also track a single master objective value that serves as a running lower bound on the overall cost assembled from earlier subproblem solutions. The objective is to minimize that master objective value.

sets.name: col
sets.doc: the candidate distillation columns of the separation superstructure; each may or may not be built

sets.name: stm
sets.doc: the two product streams leaving a column, the top stream and the bottom stream

sets.name: cp
sets.doc: the chemical components present in the feed

sets.name: k
sets.doc: the stored Benders iterations; each one contributes a cut whose coefficients were generated earlier in the decomposition run

sets.name: savkey
sets.doc: the set of feasible column-stream-component triples; lists which component is the key component recovered in each stream of each column

params.name: totfeed
params.doc: the total feed flow entering the separation system, a single scalar in flow units

params.name: zlo
params.doc: a known lower bound on the master objective value from earlier Benders iterations

params.name: nfeas
params.doc: a flag marking whether the primal subproblem at a stored iteration was feasible; 1 means feasible and 0 means infeasible

params.name: mcost
params.doc: the stored linear cost coefficient on a column's build decision for an iteration

params.name: ma1
params.doc: the stored constant part of a column's per-unit-flow cost coefficient for an iteration

params.name: ma2
params.doc: the stored recovery-sensitivity coefficient for a column stream at an iteration

params.name: ma3
params.doc: the stored composition-sensitivity coefficient for a column-component pair at an iteration

params.name: mint
params.doc: the stored logical multiplier from the feasible primal at an iteration, attached to the column linking term

params.name: mlint
params.doc: the stored logical multiplier from the relaxed primal at an infeasible iteration, attached to the column linking term

params.name: fink
params.doc: the stored inlet flow into a column recorded at an iteration, in flow units

params.name: reck
params.doc: the stored fractional key-component recovery for a column stream at an iteration; defaults to zero where not listed

params.name: xkin
params.doc: the stored inlet composition of a component into a column at an iteration, a mole fraction

params.name: cut
params.doc: the right-hand-side value of the integer cut stored at an iteration

params.name: cutcol
params.doc: the coefficient on a column's build decision in the integer cut stored at an iteration

vars.name: mu
vars.doc: the master objective value, acting as the Benders lower bound being minimized

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the build decision for each candidate column; 1 if the column is built and 0 otherwise

constraints.name: bnd
constraints.intent: The master objective value must stay at or above the known lower bound carried over from earlier in the decomposition, so the master objective is never allowed to fall below that bound.

constraints.name: nfeas[k])
constraints.intent: For each stored iteration whose primal subproblem came back feasible, add the Lagrange cut that iteration produced. The cut sums over the candidate columns a stored cost term tied to each column's build decision together with a stored per-unit-flow cost applied to that column's recorded inlet flow, and it also adds a linking term that weights, for each column, the gap between the recorded inlet flow and the total feed allocated according to the build decision. The whole sum must not exceed the master objective value. Iterations whose primal was infeasible contribute no such cut.

constraints.name: nfeas[k]))
constraints.intent: For each stored iteration whose primal subproblem came back infeasible, add the feasibility cut that iteration produced. This cut sums over the candidate columns a stored multiplier applied to the gap between each column's recorded inlet flow and the total feed allocated according to that column's build decision, and requires that sum to be at most zero. Iterations whose primal was feasible contribute no such cut, and an iteration is also dropped when all of its stored multipliers are zero so the cut would be vacuous.

constraints.name: cutcol[k,
constraints.intent: For each stored iteration, add the integer cut that rules out the build combinations already explored. The cut takes a stored coefficient on each candidate column's build decision, sums those weighted build decisions across all columns, and requires the total to be at most the stored right-hand-side value for that iteration.

query: There's a proposal on the table to swap the lead column out for the alternate: rather than scoring each candidate independently, the synthesis should only stand up the first column if it also stands up the second one. What master bound does the optimizer return once the second column gets that priority?

query: We're looking at a recovery-credit rule: the master bound should sit above a weighted score of the columns that actually get built — count 1.1 for the first candidate and 0.5 for the second, whichever the design commits to. Plug that in and tell me where mu lands.

query: Engineering is pitching a two-part scenario: commit firmly to the second candidate column, and on top of that hold the master bound to at least 1.85 whenever that second column is the one carried into the design. Walk me through the master value this package produces.

query: Whenever the first candidate column is the one we carry forward, the master bound it reports still comes in awfully thin against that column's own subproblem cost. Shouldn't committing to the first column drag the bound up to at least 1.3? What's keeping the optimizer from honoring that when it picks that column?

query: Any time the synthesis walks away from the second candidate column, the bound it falls back to looks too soft to defend in review. Couldn't we just say that skipping the second column has to come with a master bound of 1.2 or better? Walk me through what's pulling the optimizer the other way.

query: It bothers me that whichever column the design lands on, the bound never seems to reflect how costly that particular column is. Shouldn't each candidate carry its own floor on the master bound when it's the one built — say 1.15 for the first and 1.6 for the second? What's the model seeing that lets it report a bound under those per-column floors?
